enrichment.short_description: Authgear is an open-source customer identity and access management (CIAM) platform that gives developers passkeys, passwordless login, single sign-on, and multi-factor authentication as a ready-to-use service, deployable as managed cloud or self-hosted software.
enrichment.starting_price: Free plan available; paid Cloud plans start at $50/month; self-hosted Authgear ONCE starts at $299 one-time
enrichment.company_size: small team (exact headcount not publicly disclosed)
enrichment.headquarters: Hong Kong, with additional offices in Taiwan, the UK, Canada, and the US
enrichment.seo_title: Authgear Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Authgear is an open-source CIAM platform with passkeys, SSO and MFA. Compare 2026 pricing, features, pros and cons, and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Authgear is an open-source customer identity and access management (CIAM) platform built for developers who need to add authentication and authorization to web and mobile applications without building it from scratch. It is the flagship product of SkyMakers Digital Group (previously operating under the Oursky name), a bootstrapped, profitable software studio that has served enterprise and startup clients since 2009; the Authgear product itself appears to have launched around 2020 based on its public social and developer presence. Authgear is not known to have raised venture funding, and the company describes itself as deliberately independent of external investors. The platform offers passwordless and passkey login, biometric authentication, single sign-on (SSO) via SAML and OIDC, multi-factor authentication (SMS, WhatsApp, TOTP, and authenticator apps), pre-built login UI widgets, bot protection, and detailed audit logs. It can be consumed as Authgear Cloud, a fully managed SaaS offering, or self-hosted via Authgear ONCE, a one-time-purchase license that includes two years of updates. Pricing is transparent and usage-based on monthly active users (MAUs) and messaging volume: a free tier supports unlimited MAUs with limited SMS/WhatsApp messages and up to two applications, a Developers plan starts at fifty dollars per month, and a Business plan starts at five hundred dollars per month for higher usage and support tiers; Enterprise pricing is custom. Authgear holds ISO 27001 and SOC 2 Type II compliance certifications and runs on Microsoft Azure and Google Cloud infrastructure, with publicly referenced customers including Hong Kong MTR, Bupa, and Outback Steakhouse. Its target audience is engineering teams at startups and mid-market companies that want an Auth0, Firebase Authentication, or Clerk alternative with either a cloud-hosted option or full self-hosted control of their identity data, particularly teams that value open-source transparency and predictable pricing over the larger ecosystem and brand recognition of incumbents like Auth0 (Okta) or Ping Identity. Compared to those larger CIAM vendors, Authgear is smaller and has a less extensive third-party integration marketplace, but it offers a more generous free tier, simpler pricing, and the option to self-host for organizations with data residency or compliance requirements.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Authgear
paragraphs: Authgear is an open-source customer identity and access management (CIAM) platform designed to give developers a plug-and-play way to handle sign-up, login, and account security for consumer-facing web and mobile applications. Rather than building authentication logic in-house, teams integrate Authgear's SDKs and pre-built UI to get passwordless login, passkeys, social login, and multi-factor authentication out of the box.

The product is built by SkyMakers Digital Group, a software studio with roots going back to 2009 and offices spanning Hong Kong, Taiwan, the UK, Canada, and the US. The company positions itself as bootstrapped and profitable, explicitly contrasting itself with venture-backed competitors that it says must eventually prioritize growth and valuation over long-term product stability.

Authgear can be deployed two ways: as Authgear Cloud, a fully managed SaaS product, or as Authgear ONCE, a self-hosted package sold as a one-time license so organizations that need to keep identity data on their own infrastructure can still use the same core platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Authgear's core feature set covers the full authentication lifecycle: passwordless and passkey-based login, biometric authentication on supported devices, social login, and traditional email or phone plus password flows.

For organizations with more complex identity needs, it supports enterprise single sign-on through SAML and OIDC, multi-factor authentication via SMS, WhatsApp, TOTP, and authenticator apps, role- and permission-based access control, and bot or credential-stuffing protection.

Operational features include pre-built, customizable login UI widgets so teams do not need to build their own auth screens, audit logs for compliance reviews, and dashboards for managing applications, project members, and usage across environments.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Authgear Cloud offers a free plan with unlimited monthly active users, up to 100 SMS or WhatsApp messages per month, and support for two applications and two project members, making it usable for early-stage products at no cost.

The paid Developers plan starts at fifty dollars per month and adds metered SMS/WhatsApp pricing and priority email support, while the Business plan starts at five hundred dollars per month for twenty-five thousand included MAUs, five applications, longer log retention, and a dedicated Slack channel. Enterprise pricing is custom and adds data residency options, custom SMS gateways, and dedicated support.

Organizations that prefer to self-host can instead buy Authgear ONCE for a flat two hundred ninety-nine dollar one-time fee, which includes unlimited applications and MAUs plus two years of software updates; an Enterprise ONCE tier with high-availability support is available at custom pricing.

features.title: Passwordless and passkey login
features.description: Lets end users sign in with passkeys, biometrics, or magic links instead of passwords.

features.title: Enterprise SSO
features.description: Supports SAML and OIDC single sign-on for connecting to enterprise identity providers.

features.title: Multi-factor authentication
features.description: Adds SMS, WhatsApp, TOTP, and authenticator app-based second factors to any login flow.

features.title: Pre-built login UI
features.description: Ships customizable, hosted sign-up and sign-in screens so teams do not have to design their own.

features.title: Self-hosted or cloud deployment
features.description: Available as managed Authgear Cloud or as a self-hosted Authgear ONCE package for data residency needs.

features.title: Audit logs and compliance
features.description: Provides detailed audit trails and holds ISO 27001 and SOC 2 Type II certifications.

features.title: Bot and credential-stuffing protection
features.description: Includes safeguards against automated abuse of sign-up and login endpoints.

features.title: Role-based access control
features.description: Allows fine-grained permissions and roles to be attached to user accounts.

pros: Open-source codebase with a self-hosted option for full data control
Generous free tier with unlimited monthly active users
Transparent, predictable pricing compared to larger CIAM vendors
Bootstrapped and profitable, reducing pressure from investor-driven roadmap changes
ISO 27001 and SOC 2 Type II compliance for regulated industries
cons: Smaller ecosystem and integration marketplace than Auth0 or Okta
Self-hosting requires in-house DevOps and infrastructure expertise
Limited public information about company financials and team size
Fewer publicly documented enterprise case studies than larger competitors
SMS/WhatsApp messaging costs add up on higher-volume plans

faqs.question: What is Authgear used for
faqs.answer: Authgear is used by development teams to add login, sign-up, and account security to web and mobile applications without building authentication infrastructure from scratch.

faqs.question: Is Authgear open source
faqs.answer: Yes, Authgear's core server is open source, and it can also be self-hosted through the Authgear ONCE package.

faqs.question: How much does Authgear cost
faqs.answer: Authgear Cloud has a free plan, paid plans starting at fifty dollars per month, and custom Enterprise pricing; the self-hosted Authgear ONCE option starts at a one-time fee of two hundred ninety-nine dollars.

faqs.question: Does Authgear support passkeys
faqs.answer: Yes, Authgear supports passkeys and biometric login alongside traditional password and social login options.

faqs.question: Who owns Authgear
faqs.answer: Authgear is built and operated by SkyMakers Digital Group, a bootstrapped software studio with offices in Hong Kong, Taiwan, the UK, Canada, and the US.

faqs.question: Is Authgear a good Auth0 alternative
faqs.answer: Authgear is commonly positioned as an Auth0 or Firebase Authentication alternative, offering similar core authentication features with simpler pricing and a self-hosting option.

faqs.question: Can Authgear handle enterprise SSO
faqs.answer: Yes, Authgear supports SAML and OIDC-based single sign-on for connecting business applications to enterprise identity providers.

faqs.question: Does Authgear have a free tier
faqs.answer: Yes, Authgear Cloud offers a free plan with unlimited monthly active users and limited SMS or WhatsApp messaging.

use_cases.title: Startup adding login to a new app
use_cases.description: Early-stage teams use Authgear's free tier to launch passwordless and social login quickly without dedicating engineering time to building auth.

use_cases.title: Regulated company needing self-hosted identity
use_cases.description: Organizations with data residency or compliance requirements deploy Authgear ONCE on their own infrastructure while keeping the same feature set as the cloud product.
use_cases.audience: Regulated industries such as healthcare and finance

use_cases.title: Enterprise consolidating customer logins
use_cases.description: Mid-market and enterprise companies use Authgear's SSO and MFA features to unify login across multiple customer-facing applications.

enrichment.short_description: AuthPass is a free, open-source, cross-platform password manager built with Flutter that is fully compatible with KeePass KDBX 3 and KDBX 4 database files.
enrichment.company_size: independent open-source project maintained by its creator and community contributors
enrichment.headquarters: Austria (creator's location); distributed open-source contributor community
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AuthPass is a free open-source password manager built with Flutter, compatible with KeePass KDBX files. See features, pricing, and pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: AuthPass is a free, open-source, cross-platform password manager built with the Flutter framework, first released around September 2019 by Herbert Poul, a freelance mobile and web developer based in Austria. The project is now maintained by Poul along with a group of open-source community contributors on GitHub, and it is distributed under the GPL-3.0 license. AuthPass is not a venture-backed company; there is no public record of institutional funding, and it operates as an independent open-source project rather than a commercial entity with disclosed revenue or headcount. Its defining feature is full compatibility with KeePass database files (both KDBX 3 and KDBX 4 formats), which lets users who already store passwords in KeePass, KeePassXC, or similar tools open and edit the same encrypted database from AuthPass on any supported platform. The app runs on Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, and iOS, plus a web-based edition that works in any modern browser, and it supports biometric unlock (fingerprint or face recognition) backed by the operating system's secure keystore or keychain. For syncing, AuthPass relies on existing cloud storage providers such as Google Drive, Dropbox, and WebDAV-compatible services like Nextcloud and ownCloud, rather than operating its own proprietary sync backend. Because the core app and its source code are free and open, AuthPass has no subscription tiers or paid plans in the traditional sense; its pricing model is effectively free and open-source software, with the project sustained through community contributions and, at various points, an associated AuthPass Cloud sync offering. Its target audience is privacy-conscious individuals and technical users who want a self-controlled, standards-based password manager rather than a closed-source SaaS vault, and specifically people already invested in the KeePass ecosystem who want a modern, Flutter-based client. Compared to commercial alternatives like 1Password, LastPass, or Bitwarden, AuthPass trades built-in cloud sync and dedicated customer support for open-source transparency, no subscription cost, and direct compatibility with the widely used KDBX file format.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is AuthPass
paragraphs: AuthPass is a free, open-source password manager built with Google's Flutter framework, which lets it run natively across Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS, and the web from a single codebase. It was first released around September 2019 by Herbert Poul, a freelance developer based in Austria, and has since been maintained with help from open-source contributors.

The defining characteristic of AuthPass is that it works directly with KeePass-format database files, specifically KDBX 3 and KDBX 4. This means users are not locked into a proprietary vault format controlled by a single vendor; the same encrypted database file can be opened in AuthPass, KeePassXC, or other KeePass-compatible clients.
The project's source code is publicly available on GitHub under the GPL-3.0 license, and the app is distributed through the App Store, Google Play, and direct downloads for desktop platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: AuthPass loads and decrypts KDBX files using a master password and, optionally, a key file for an added layer of protection. Once unlocked, the app can store the master password in the device's secure keystore or keychain and let users unlock the vault afterward with a fingerprint or face scan.

Because AuthPass does not run its own proprietary sync service by default, it integrates with existing cloud storage providers, including Google Drive, Dropbox, and WebDAV-compatible services such as Nextcloud and ownCloud, so vaults stay synchronized across devices.

Other features include password generation, organization of entries into groups and tags, attachment support inherited from the KDBX format, and a consistent interface across desktop, mobile, and web so users have the same experience regardless of platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: AuthPass itself is free and open-source software with no license fee, subscription, or paid tier for the core app; anyone can download the source from GitHub and build or run it without payment.

There are no publicly listed pricing plans because AuthPass does not operate a commercial SaaS pricing model in the way closed-source competitors do; its sustainability instead comes from voluntary community contributions and the goodwill of its maintainers.

Users who want cloud sync typically bring their own storage account (Google Drive, Dropbox, or a WebDAV server), which may carry its own separate cost from the storage provider, but this is unrelated to AuthPass's own pricing.

features.title: KeePass KDBX compatibility
features.description: Opens and saves standard KDBX 3 and KDBX 4 database files used by KeePass and KeePassXC.

features.title: Cross-platform Flutter app
features.description: Runs on Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS, and the web from a single codebase.

features.title: Biometric unlock
features.description: Uses the device keystore or keychain to unlock vaults with fingerprint or face recognition.

features.title: Cloud storage sync
features.description: Syncs vault files via Google Drive, Dropbox, or WebDAV services like Nextcloud.

features.title: Open-source codebase
features.description: Source code is publicly available on GitHub under the GPL-3.0 license.

features.title: Password generator
features.description: Built-in generator for creating strong, random passwords for new entries.

features.title: Key file support
features.description: Supports an optional key file in addition to the master password for extra vault security.

pros: Completely free with no subscription cost
Open-source code that can be independently audited
Full compatibility with the widely used KeePass KDBX format
Works across desktop, mobile, and web on a single vault
No vendor lock-in since users control their own database file and storage
cons: No proprietary built-in cloud sync, requiring a third-party storage account
Relies on volunteer maintenance rather than a dedicated support team
Less polished enterprise features compared to commercial password managers
Smaller community and slower feature velocity than large funded competitors

faqs.question: Is AuthPass free
faqs.answer: Yes, AuthPass is completely free and open-source, with no subscription or license fee for the core application.

faqs.question: Is AuthPass compatible with KeePass
faqs.answer: Yes, AuthPass reads and writes standard KeePass KDBX 3 and KDBX 4 database files, so it can open vaults created in KeePass or KeePassXC.

faqs.question: Who makes AuthPass
faqs.answer: AuthPass was created by Herbert Poul, a freelance developer based in Austria, and is maintained with community contributions on GitHub.

faqs.question: Does AuthPass sync across devices
faqs.answer: AuthPass does not run its own sync service; instead it syncs vault files through connected cloud storage such as Google Drive, Dropbox, or WebDAV.

faqs.question: What platforms does AuthPass support
faqs.answer: AuthPass runs on Windows, macOS, Linux, Android, iOS, and in the browser through a web edition.

faqs.question: Is AuthPass open source
faqs.answer: Yes, AuthPass's source code is publicly hosted on GitHub and distributed under the GPL-3.0 license.

faqs.question: Can AuthPass use biometric login
faqs.answer: Yes, on supported devices AuthPass can store the master password in the secure keystore or keychain and unlock via fingerprint or face recognition.

use_cases.title: Existing KeePass users switching clients
use_cases.description: Users with an existing KDBX vault can open it directly in AuthPass without migrating or converting data.

use_cases.title: Privacy-conscious individuals avoiding subscriptions
use_cases.description: People who want a password manager without an ongoing subscription fee or a closed proprietary vault format use AuthPass as a free alternative.

use_cases.title: Cross-platform household or small team
use_cases.description: Families or small teams share a single KDBX vault synced through Dropbox or Google Drive and access it from AuthPass on any device.

enrichment.short_description: Authress is a developer-focused login and access control API that combines authentication with fine-grained, resource-based authorization, billed on a pay-per-call basis.
enrichment.starting_price: $0.0012 per API call after the first 1,000 free calls per month
enrichment.company_size: small team (exact headcount not publicly disclosed); described as an unfunded, independently operated company
enrichment.seo_title: Authress Review 2026: Pricing, Features and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Authress is a pay-per-call authentication and authorization API. See 2026 pricing, features, pros and cons, and how it compares to Auth0.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Authress is a developer-focused API that combines user authentication with fine-grained, resource-based authorization, allowing engineering teams to manage both who a user is and exactly what they are allowed to access from a single service. It is operated by Rhosys AG and, according to third-party company databases, was founded in 2018 by Dorota Parad and is headquartered in Winterthur, Switzerland; it is described as an unfunded, independently operated company with no disclosed institutional investment. Authress differentiates itself from many identity providers by emphasizing authorization as a first-class concern rather than an afterthought: it issues JWTs, manages user sign-up and sign-in flows, and layers on granular, resource-level access control so that permissions can be scoped to specific records or objects rather than just broad roles. Supported capabilities include unlimited single sign-on connections across SAML, OAuth, and OIDC, social login, machine-to-machine tokens, enterprise sign-up and invitation flows, and a claimed 99.995 percent availability SLA. Pricing is usage-based and unusually transparent for the identity category: Authress charges 0.0012 US dollars per billable authentication or authorization API call, with the first 1,000 calls each month free, and the company markets this as having no enterprise tax, meaning all customers get the same feature set regardless of spend rather than gating capabilities like SSO or advanced permissions behind higher-priced tiers. There are no separate named pricing plans beyond this single pay-per-use structure. Authress's target audience is engineering teams, particularly at startups and mid-sized software companies, that need both authentication and granular authorization in one integration and want to avoid the cost escalation and feature-gating common with larger identity platforms. It is most directly compared to Auth0, Okta, and similar CIAM vendors, but its narrower focus on combining login with resource-based access control also puts it in the same conversation as dedicated authorization services. Because Authress is a smaller, independently run company, prospective users should weigh its transparent pricing and authorization-first design against the larger support organizations, integration marketplaces, and enterprise track records of bigger competitors.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Authress
paragraphs: Authress is a cloud-based API that provides both authentication and authorization for applications, aimed specifically at developers who need fine-grained control over what authenticated users are permitted to do, not just who they are. It is operated by Rhosys AG and, per public company databases, was founded in 2018 by Dorota Parad, with the company based in Winterthur, Switzerland.

Unlike identity providers that treat authorization as a secondary, role-based add-on, Authress is built around resource-based access control from the start, letting teams assign permissions down to individual records or objects rather than only broad application-wide roles.

Authress positions itself as an unfunded, independently operated alternative to larger CIAM vendors, and it emphasizes a single, transparent pay-per-call pricing model rather than tiered plans that gate features behind higher-priced tiers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Authress issues JSON Web Tokens (JWTs) for authenticated sessions and supports unlimited single sign-on connections across SAML, OAuth, and OIDC, plus social login and machine-to-machine tokens for service-to-service authentication.

Its authorization layer supports granular, resource-based permission checks, enterprise sign-up and invitation flows, and multi-language SDK support (the company references support across 26-plus languages for its client libraries and documentation).
Operationally, Authress advertises a 99.995 percent availability SLA and encrypts data both in transit and at rest, targeting production applications that need reliable, low-latency access decisions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Authress uses a single usage-based pricing model rather than multiple named plans: customers pay 0.0012 US dollars per billable API call, with only actual authentication and authorization calls counted toward billing.
The first 1,000 billable calls each month are free, which lets small projects and prototypes run at no cost before usage-based billing kicks in.

The company describes this as having no enterprise tax, meaning every customer, regardless of size or spend, gets access to the same full feature set, including SSO, social login, and fine-grained authorization, without needing to upgrade to a higher tier to unlock them.

features.title: Resource-based authorization
features.description: Grants permissions scoped to individual resources or records, not just broad application roles.

features.title: Unified authentication and authorization
features.description: Combines user login flows with access control decisions in a single API.

features.title: Enterprise SSO connections
features.description: Supports unlimited SAML, OAuth, and OIDC single sign-on connections.

features.title: Social login and machine tokens
features.description: Supports social identity providers as well as machine-to-machine authentication tokens.

features.title: Multi-language SDKs
features.description: Provides client libraries and documentation across a wide range of programming languages.

features.title: High availability
features.description: Advertises a 99.995 percent availability SLA for production workloads.

features.title: Enterprise invitation flows
features.description: Supports built-in sign-up and invitation workflows for onboarding organizational users.

features.title: Pay-per-call pricing
features.description: Bills only for actual authentication and authorization calls with no feature-gated tiers.

pros: Transparent, single pay-per-call pricing model with no feature-gated tiers
Fine-grained, resource-based authorization built in rather than bolted on
Free allotment of 1,000 calls per month for small projects
Unlimited SSO connections included at every usage level
Simple integration model for teams that need both authentication and authorization
cons: Smaller, independently run company compared to major CIAM vendors
Limited public information on company size, funding, and long-term roadmap
Usage-based pricing can be harder to forecast at very high call volumes than flat tiers
Smaller partner and integration ecosystem than Auth0 or Okta

faqs.question: What does Authress do
faqs.answer: Authress provides a combined authentication and authorization API, handling both user login and fine-grained, resource-based access control decisions.

faqs.question: How is Authress priced
faqs.answer: Authress uses pay-per-call pricing at 0.0012 US dollars per billable API call, with the first 1,000 calls each month free.

faqs.question: Who founded Authress
faqs.answer: According to public company records, Authress was founded in 2018 by Dorota Parad and is based in Winterthur, Switzerland, operated by Rhosys AG.

faqs.question: Is Authress an Auth0 alternative
faqs.answer: Yes, Authress is commonly compared to Auth0 as an authentication and authorization alternative, with a stronger emphasis on fine-grained authorization.

faqs.question: Does Authress support single sign-on
faqs.answer: Yes, Authress supports unlimited SAML, OAuth, and OIDC single sign-on connections on every plan.

faqs.question: Does Authress have a free tier
faqs.answer: Yes, the first 1,000 billable API calls each month are free before usage-based billing applies.

faqs.question: What is resource-based authorization
faqs.answer: Resource-based authorization assigns permissions to specific resources or records rather than only broad, application-wide roles, allowing more precise access control.

use_cases.title: SaaS app needing granular permissions
use_cases.description: Multi-tenant SaaS products use Authress to assign per-resource permissions so customers can control exactly what their own users can access.

use_cases.title: Startup replacing custom auth code
use_cases.description: Early-stage companies use Authress instead of building and maintaining their own authentication and permission logic in-house.

use_cases.title: Enterprise integration with existing identity providers
use_cases.description: Organizations connect their existing SAML or OIDC identity providers to Authress to centralize access control across multiple applications.

enrichment.short_description: Authy is a two-factor authentication (2FA) mobile app owned by Twilio; its desktop apps were discontinued in 2024, and Twilio has scaled back investment in the consumer product while steering developers toward its Twilio Verify API.
enrichment.company_size: operated as a product within Twilio, a public company with over 5,000 employees
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco, California (Twilio headquarters); Authy was originally founded in Colombia before relocating
enrichment.seo_title: Authy Status in 2026: Is It Still Supported? Full Review
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Authy is Twilio's 2FA app. See its 2026 status after the desktop app shutdown, current features, pricing, and the best alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Authy is a two-factor authentication (2FA) mobile application originally founded around 2011 to 2012, reportedly starting in Colombia before relocating to California, by Daniel Palacio. It raised early seed funding, including backing from Y Combinator, before Twilio acquired it in February 2015 for an undisclosed amount; financial terms of the acquisition were never publicly released. Authy generates time-based one-time passcodes (TOTP) and supports push-based authentication, offering features such as encrypted cloud backup of tokens, multi-device synchronization, and offline code generation, and it has historically been offered free to consumers while Twilio separately monetized the underlying Authy API and, later, its Verify API for business customers. As of this writing, Authy's status has changed materially since its early years: Twilio discontinued the Authy desktop applications for Windows, macOS, and Linux, with end-of-life on March 19, 2024, forcing all desktop users to migrate to the mobile-only app. Twilio has publicly stated it is streamlining focus toward higher-demand products, and industry coverage describes Authy's mobile app as being in a reduced-investment or maintenance-oriented state, with Twilio directing developers toward its separate Verify API rather than the legacy Authy API for new integrations. As of the time of this research, Twilio has stated it intends to continue supporting the Authy mobile apps for existing users and as a consumer 2FA option, but no fixed long-term roadmap or feature-development commitment has been publicly confirmed, and security commentators increasingly recommend evaluating alternatives such as Aegis, Ente Auth, 2FAS, or Microsoft Authenticator, particularly given Authy's historical lack of an official token-export feature. Given this trajectory, prospective users should treat Authy as a legacy consumer 2FA app that remains functional but is not clearly under active feature development, rather than as a fully deprecated or fully thriving product; readers should verify Authy's current app store availability and any new Twilio announcements before relying on it for new 2FA setups.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Authy
paragraphs: Authy is a two-factor authentication (2FA) mobile app that generates time-based one-time passcodes and supports push-notification-based login approval for online accounts. It was founded around 2011 to 2012, with early roots reportedly in Colombia before the company moved to California, and it grew with seed backing that included Y Combinator before being acquired by Twilio in February 2015 for an undisclosed sum.

Historically, Authy differentiated itself from competitors like Google Authenticator through encrypted cloud backup and multi-device synchronization, letting users recover or transfer their 2FA tokens across phones without losing access to accounts.

Since the Twilio acquisition, Authy has functioned as both a free consumer app and the branding for Twilio's underlying developer authentication API, though Twilio has since encouraged business customers to adopt its newer Verify API instead of the original Authy API for new integrations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Current Status: Desktop Shutdown and Reduced Investment
paragraphs: Twilio discontinued the Authy desktop applications for Windows, macOS, and Linux, with the official end-of-life date set for March 19, 2024. Desktop users were logged out and instructed to migrate all of their 2FA tokens to the mobile-only app, a process complicated by Authy's lack of a built-in export feature.

Coverage of the shutdown indicates Twilio made the decision to concentrate development resources on higher-demand areas of its business, which several industry outlets have interpreted as a broader deprioritization of the consumer-facing Authy product in favor of Twilio's enterprise-focused Verify API.

Twilio has stated it intends to keep the Authy mobile apps available for existing users and as a consumer option, and as of this research the mobile apps remain downloadable and functional, but there has been no confirmed public commitment to ongoing feature development. Readers evaluating Authy today should check Twilio's current changelog and app store listings directly, since the product's investment level has visibly declined and its long-term roadmap has not been clearly reaffirmed.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Authy mobile app is free for consumers and always has been; there is no subscription or paid tier for end users generating 2FA codes.

Twilio separately charges businesses for the developer-facing side of two-factor authentication through its Verify API, which is usage-based and billed per verification, but this is distinct from the free consumer Authy app.
Because Authy itself has no consumer pricing plans, there is no public pricing_plans structure to list beyond noting the app is free to download and use.

features.title: TOTP code generation
features.description: Generates standard time-based one-time passcodes compatible with most services that support 2FA apps.

features.title: Push-based authentication
features.description: Supports one-tap push approval for login requests on services that integrate with Authy.

features.title: Encrypted cloud backup
features.description: Backs up 2FA tokens in encrypted form so they can be restored on a new device.

features.title: Multi-device sync
features.description: Synchronizes tokens across multiple phones and tablets tied to the same account.

features.title: PIN and biometric app lock
features.description: Protects the app itself with a PIN code or device biometrics.

features.title: QR code setup
features.description: Adds new accounts by scanning standard QR codes presented during 2FA setup.

pros: Free to use for consumers
Encrypted backup and multi-device sync, unlike many bare-bones 2FA apps
Backed by Twilio, a large, publicly traded communications company
Widely supported by major websites and services for 2FA setup
Push-approval option in addition to standard TOTP codes
cons: Desktop apps were permanently discontinued in March 2024
Reduced investment and unclear long-term roadmap for the mobile app
No official built-in export feature, making migration to another app difficult
Requires a phone number and cloud backup, which some privacy-focused users avoid
Twilio is steering developer efforts toward its separate Verify API rather than the legacy Authy API

faqs.question: Is Authy still supported in 2026
faqs.answer: The Authy mobile app remains available and functional, but Twilio discontinued the desktop apps in March 2024 and has visibly reduced investment in the consumer product, so users should verify current status directly with Twilio before relying on it long term.

faqs.question: Why did Authy shut down its desktop app
faqs.answer: Twilio stated it wanted to concentrate development resources on higher-demand areas of its business, and ended support for the Windows, macOS, and Linux desktop apps on March 19, 2024.

faqs.question: Who owns Authy
faqs.answer: Authy is owned by Twilio, which acquired the company in February 2015 for an undisclosed amount.

faqs.question: Is Authy free
faqs.answer: Yes, the Authy mobile app is free for consumers to download and use for two-factor authentication.

faqs.question: Can I export my Authy tokens
faqs.answer: Authy has historically lacked an official export feature, which makes migrating to a different 2FA app a manual process of disabling and re-enrolling each account.

faqs.question: What are good Authy alternatives
faqs.answer: Commonly recommended alternatives include Aegis, Ente Auth, 2FAS, and Microsoft Authenticator, several of which offer open-source code or easier data export.

faqs.question: Does Authy still work on desktop
faqs.answer: No, Twilio ended support for Authy's Windows, macOS, and Linux desktop apps in March 2024; the product is now mobile-only.

faqs.question: Is Authy the same as Twilio Verify
faqs.answer: No, Authy is the original consumer 2FA app and developer API Twilio acquired in 2015, while Twilio Verify is a newer, separate API that Twilio now recommends to developers for building authentication into their own products.

use_cases.title: Consumer securing personal accounts
use_cases.description: Individuals use the free Authy app to generate 2FA codes for services like exchanges, email, and social media accounts.

use_cases.title: User migrating from Authy desktop
use_cases.description: Former desktop app users manually re-enroll their accounts on the mobile-only app following the 2024 desktop shutdown.
use_cases.audience: Existing Authy users affected by the desktop EOL

use_cases.title: Developer evaluating 2FA options
use_cases.description: Developers researching two-factor authentication for their own products compare the legacy Authy API against Twilio's newer Verify API before choosing an integration path.

enrichment.short_description: Autodesk is a large public software company that makes design and engineering applications, including AutoCAD, Revit, Maya, and Fusion, used across architecture, engineering, construction, manufacturing, and media and entertainment.
enrichment.starting_price: Varies by product; Fusion starts free for personal use, with paid subscriptions such as Revit at roughly $2,915 per year for a single named-user license
enrichment.seo_title: Autodesk Review 2026: Products, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Autodesk makes AutoCAD, Revit, Maya and Fusion. See 2026 pricing, key products, pros and cons for architecture, engineering and design teams.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Autodesk is a large, publicly traded American software company (NASDAQ: ADSK) that develops design, engineering, and creative software used across architecture, engineering, construction (AEC), manufacturing, and media and entertainment industries. It was founded in April 1982 by John Walker, Daniel Drake, and a group of other programmers with an initial investment of roughly sixty thousand dollars; the company's first major product, AutoCAD, released in 1982, became one of the earliest widely adopted computer-aided design (CAD) programs for personal computers. Autodesk was headquartered in San Rafael, California, from 1994 until 2022, when it moved its official headquarters to San Francisco. As a mature public company, Autodesk does not disclose venture funding figures since it has been publicly traded since 1985; recent public filings put annual revenue in the range of several billion dollars (approximately 6.78 billion dollars, per third-party financial trackers) and employee headcount at roughly 15,000 to 15,300, though the company announced workforce reductions of about seven percent (roughly 1,000 roles) as part of a 2026 restructuring. Autodesk's product portfolio spans multiple flagship applications rather than a single tool: AutoCAD for general-purpose 2D and 3D drafting, Revit for building information modeling (BIM) in architecture and construction, Civil 3D for civil engineering, Maya and 3ds Max for 3D animation and visual effects used heavily in film and games, and Fusion (formerly Fusion 360) for cloud-based mechanical design, simulation, and manufacturing. Nearly all products are sold via subscription, typically billed monthly, annually, or on multi-year terms, with bundled offerings like the Architecture, Engineering and Construction (AEC) Collection combining several tools at a discount versus buying them individually. Fusion offers a limited free tier for personal, non-commercial use. Autodesk's target audience ranges from individual freelance designers and students (who can access free educational licenses) up to large enterprise engineering, construction, and media production companies. Its main competitors vary by product line and include Dassault Systemes (SolidWorks, CATIA), Bentley Systems, Trimble (SketchUp), PTC, and Blender (as a free alternative for 3D animation), with Autodesk generally positioned as the entrenched, feature-complete incumbent across most of the categories it competes in.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Autodesk
paragraphs: Autodesk is a publicly traded American software company that builds design and engineering tools used across architecture, engineering, construction, product manufacturing, and media and entertainment. Rather than a single application, Autodesk is best understood as a portfolio company whose products, including AutoCAD, Revit, Maya, 3ds Max, Civil 3D, and Fusion, each serve different professional design workflows.

The company was founded in April 1982 by John Walker, Daniel Drake, and a group of other programmers, and it built its early business around AutoCAD, one of the first computer-aided design programs available on personal computers. Autodesk has been publicly traded since 1985 and trades on NASDAQ under the ticker ADSK.

Autodesk's headquarters moved from San Rafael, California, where it had been based since 1994, to San Francisco in 2022. The company today serves millions of professional and student users worldwide through a mix of individual subscriptions, multi-product collections, and large enterprise agreements.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Products and Features
paragraphs: AutoCAD remains Autodesk's flagship general-purpose drafting tool for 2D and 3D design across industries, while Revit focuses specifically on building information modeling (BIM) for architecture, structural, and MEP (mechanical, electrical, plumbing) engineering teams designing buildings.

For manufacturing and product design, Fusion combines CAD, computer-aided manufacturing (CAM), and simulation in a cloud-connected platform, while Maya and 3ds Max serve 3D animation, modeling, and visual effects work common in film, television, and game development.

Autodesk also offers Civil 3D for civil engineering and infrastructure design, Navisworks for construction project review and clash detection, and Autodesk Construction Cloud, a broader platform for coordinating design and construction data across project teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Autodesk sells almost all of its products by subscription, typically available as monthly, annual, or three-year commitments, with longer terms offering a lower effective monthly cost. Pricing varies significantly by product and industry collection.

As an example, a single named-user Revit subscription runs approximately 2,915 US dollars per year (roughly 380 dollars per month), while the broader AEC Collection, which bundles Revit with AutoCAD, Civil 3D, Navisworks, and other tools, runs approximately 3,375 to 3,795 dollars per year depending on the source and current promotions. Fusion is priced separately, with paid subscriptions around 680 dollars per year, alongside a free tier for personal, non-commercial use with reduced functionality.

Because pricing changes periodically and varies by region, product edition, and any active promotions, prospective buyers should confirm current prices directly on Autodesk's official pricing pages before purchasing.

features.title: AutoCAD 2D/3D drafting
features.description: General-purpose computer-aided design software used across architecture, engineering, and manufacturing.

features.title: Revit building information modeling
features.description: BIM software purpose-built for architecture, structural, and MEP building design and coordination.

features.title: Fusion CAD/CAM/simulation
features.description: Cloud-connected platform combining mechanical design, manufacturing, and simulation tools.

features.title: Maya and 3ds Max 3D animation
features.description: Industry-standard tools for 3D modeling, animation, and visual effects in film, TV, and games.

features.title: Civil 3D infrastructure design
features.description: Specialized tools for civil engineering, grading, and infrastructure project design.

features.title: Autodesk Construction Cloud
features.description: Platform connecting design and construction teams around shared project data.

features.title: Cloud collaboration
features.description: Cloud-based file sharing and real-time collaboration across most modern Autodesk products.

features.title: Educational licensing
features.description: Free access to most Autodesk software for verified students and educators.

pros: Industry-standard tools with deep feature sets across multiple design disciplines
Broad ecosystem of third-party plugins, training, and certified professionals
Free educational licenses lower the barrier for students to learn on real tools
Regularly updated with cloud collaboration and simulation capabilities
Bundled collections can reduce per-product cost for teams needing multiple tools
cons: Subscription costs are high compared to some newer competitors and open-source alternatives
Mandatory subscription model since discontinuing perpetual licenses has drawn criticism
Steep learning curve for full-featured products like Revit and Maya
Overlapping products across the portfolio can make purchasing decisions confusing
Frequent price increases and licensing changes over the years

faqs.question: What does Autodesk make
faqs.answer: Autodesk makes design and engineering software including AutoCAD, Revit, Maya, 3ds Max, Civil 3D, and Fusion, used in architecture, engineering, construction, manufacturing, and media.

faqs.question: When was Autodesk founded
faqs.answer: Autodesk was founded in April 1982 by John Walker, Daniel Drake, and a group of other programmers.

faqs.question: Where is Autodesk headquartered
faqs.answer: Autodesk is headquartered in San Francisco, California, having moved there from San Rafael, California, in 2022.

faqs.question: Is Autodesk software subscription-based
faqs.answer: Yes, Autodesk sells nearly all of its products by monthly, annual, or multi-year subscription rather than perpetual license.

faqs.question: Is there a free version of Autodesk software
faqs.answer: Fusion offers a free tier for personal, non-commercial use, and most Autodesk products are available free to verified students and educators.

faqs.question: What is the difference between AutoCAD and Revit
faqs.answer: AutoCAD is general-purpose 2D and 3D drafting software, while Revit is purpose-built for building information modeling (BIM) in architecture and construction.

faqs.question: Is Autodesk a public company
faqs.answer: Yes, Autodesk has been publicly traded since 1985 and trades on NASDAQ under the ticker ADSK.

faqs.question: Who are Autodesk's main competitors
faqs.answer: Depending on the product line, competitors include Dassault Systemes (SolidWorks, CATIA), Bentley Systems, Trimble (SketchUp), PTC, and Blender as a free alternative for 3D animation.

enrichment.short_description: AutoLocalise is an AI-powered localization platform that automatically translates app and website text in real time into over 100 languages without requiring developers to manage traditional translation files.
enrichment.starting_price: Free tier available; paid Pro plan starts at $9/month
enrichment.company_size: small startup team (exact headcount not publicly disclosed)
enrichment.seo_title: AutoLocalise Review 2026: AI Localization Pricing and Features
enrichment.seo_meta_description: AutoLocalise is an AI localization tool for React, Next.js and Python apps. See 2026 pricing, features, pros and cons versus Lokalise and Crowdin.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: AutoLocalise is an AI-powered, real-time localization platform designed to let developers add multi-language support to web and mobile applications without managing traditional translation files or manual localization workflows. It targets developers building with React, Next.js, React Native, Expo, and Python, offering an SDK that automatically extracts UI text and translates it into more than 100 languages on the fly, with translations updating instantly without requiring an app rebuild or redeployment. The company's public founding date, funding history, and exact headquarters location are not publicly disclosed in available sources, and it appears to operate as a small, independently run startup rather than a venture-backed company with disclosed financials; readers should treat any specific founding year or funding figure as unconfirmed unless verified directly with the company. AutoLocalise markets itself heavily on cost savings versus established localization platforms like Lokalise and Crowdin, with case studies on its own site describing teams cutting spend from thousands of dollars per month down to a nine-dollar Pro plan. Key product capabilities include AI-driven translation that improves from manual corrections over time, local caching of translations for offline performance, usage analytics to track translation activity, and the ability to share translation projects across multiple applications. Its pricing follows a freemium, usage-based model: a free tier includes 10,000 translated characters per month and one project, while the Pro plan costs nine dollars per month for 250,000 characters, unlimited projects, and priority support, with additional character bundles and per-seat charges for extra team members available beyond those limits. AutoLocalise's target audience is engineering teams, particularly at startups and small to mid-sized software companies, that want to internationalize a product quickly without hiring a localization team or maintaining .json or .po translation files by hand. Compared to established competitors like Lokalise, Crowdin, and Phrase, which offer more mature translation management workflows, human-in-the-loop review, and enterprise governance features, AutoLocalise trades some of that process maturity for a much lower price point and a faster, more automated, code-light integration path.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is AutoLocalise
paragraphs: AutoLocalise is a localization platform that uses AI to translate application and website text in real time, aiming to remove the traditional overhead of managing translation files and localization workflows. Instead of exporting strings to translation files and re-importing them after human translation, developers install a lightweight SDK that automatically detects and translates UI text as users interact with the app.

The platform supports popular frameworks including React, Next.js, React Native, Expo, and Python-based applications, and it promises translation into over 100 languages with updates that apply instantly, without requiring a new app build or deployment.

AutoLocalise appears to be a small, independently operated company; specific details about its founding year, funding history, and headquarters are not publicly confirmed in available sources, so those details should be verified directly with the company before being treated as fact.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The core of AutoLocalise's product is AI-powered translation that automatically extracts text from an application's interface and translates it with context awareness, improving over time as users make manual corrections to specific translations.

Translations update in real time without requiring developers to rebuild or redeploy their application, and the SDK caches translations locally on the client for fast performance and to support offline use.

Additional platform features include usage analytics for tracking translation activity and performance, and the ability to share and reuse translation projects across multiple applications within the same account.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: AutoLocalise offers a free tier that includes 10,000 translated characters per month and one project, suitable for testing the product or very small applications.

The Pro plan costs nine dollars per month and raises the limit to 250,000 characters per month with unlimited projects and priority support, positioning AutoLocalise significantly below the cost of many established localization platforms for teams with moderate translation volume.

Beyond the included character allowance, AutoLocalise charges additional usage fees, roughly one dollar per 30,000 extra characters, along with a per-seat charge of about two dollars per month for each additional team member added to an account.

features.title: Real-time AI translation
features.description: Automatically translates UI text into over 100 languages without manual translation file management.

features.title: Instant updates without rebuilds
features.description: New or changed translations apply immediately without requiring an app rebuild or redeploy.

features.title: Framework SDK support
features.description: Provides SDKs for React, Next.js, React Native, Expo, and Python applications.

features.title: Automatic text extraction
features.description: Detects and extracts UI text that needs translation directly from the application interface.

features.title: Local caching
features.description: Caches translations on the client for fast performance and offline availability.

features.title: Usage analytics
features.description: Tracks translation volume and performance metrics for optimization.

features.title: Shared projects
features.description: Allows translation data to be shared and reused across multiple applications.

pros: Significantly lower cost than established localization platforms for small to mid-volume use
Fast integration with minimal code changes required
No manual translation file management or rebuild-and-redeploy cycle
Free tier available for testing or small projects
Supports popular modern frameworks like React, Next.js, and React Native
cons: Company founding date, funding, and headquarters are not publicly disclosed
Less mature translation management and human review workflows than established competitors
AI-generated translations may require manual correction for quality and context
Smaller ecosystem and community compared to platforms like Lokalise or Crowdin
Character-based pricing can add unpredictable costs at high translation volumes

faqs.question: What is AutoLocalise used for
faqs.answer: AutoLocalise is used by developers to add real-time, AI-powered translation to web and mobile applications without managing traditional translation files.

faqs.question: How much does AutoLocalise cost
faqs.answer: AutoLocalise has a free tier with 10,000 characters per month, and a Pro plan starting at nine dollars per month for 250,000 characters.

faqs.question: What frameworks does AutoLocalise support
faqs.answer: AutoLocalise supports React, Next.js, React Native, Expo, and Python-based applications.

faqs.question: Does AutoLocalise require translation files
faqs.answer: No, AutoLocalise is designed to eliminate the need for traditional translation files by automatically extracting and translating UI text through its SDK.

faqs.question: How many languages does AutoLocalise support
faqs.answer: AutoLocalise advertises support for translating into over 100 languages.

faqs.question: Is AutoLocalise a good alternative to Lokalise or Crowdin
faqs.answer: AutoLocalise is often marketed as a lower-cost alternative to Lokalise and Crowdin for teams with moderate translation needs, though it offers less mature translation management workflows.

faqs.question: Does AutoLocalise work offline
faqs.answer: AutoLocalise caches translations locally on the client, which supports offline availability of previously translated content.

use_cases.title: Startup launching an app internationally

use_cases.title: Team replacing an expensive localization platform
use_cases.description: Companies with growing translation costs on platforms like Lokalise or Crowdin migrate to AutoLocalise to reduce monthly spend.
use_cases.audience: Product and engineering teams with existing localization tools

use_cases.title: Developer adding translations without file management
use_cases.description: Individual developers use AutoLocalise's SDK to translate an app's interface without maintaining separate translation files per language.

enrichment.short_description: Autopilot was a visual marketing automation platform that has since been fully rebranded as Ortto; the Autopilot name and autopilothq.com domain are retired, and the product now operates under the Ortto brand.
enrichment.starting_price: Not applicable under the Autopilot brand; current product is sold as Ortto (pricing set by Ortto, not covered here)
enrichment.company_size: Now operates as Ortto; historical Autopilot team size not publicly disclosed
enrichment.headquarters: Historically San Francisco, California and Sydney, Australia; now operates as Ortto
enrichment.seo_title: Autopilot Marketing Automation: What Happened, Now Ortto
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Autopilot marketing automation was rebranded as Ortto in 2022. See its history, what changed, and where to find the product today.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Autopilot was a visual, drag-and-drop marketing automation platform originally built to help marketing teams design customer journeys, email campaigns, and lead-nurturing workflows without writing code. The company was founded around 2011 and built a customer base using a distinctive canvas-style journey builder. In 2021 the company began rebuilding its product with expanded customer data and analytics capabilities beyond marketing automation alone, releasing the rebuilt platform in beta that year, and in March 2022 it completed a full rebrand from Autopilot to Ortto, retiring the Autopilot name and the autopilothq.com domain, which now redirects to Ortto's site. According to the company's own public explanation, the rebrand reflected an evolution beyond the original journey-builder product into a broader customer data, messaging, and analytics platform, rather than a shutdown or asset sale. Available public sources for this research did not surface a confirmed acquisition of Autopilot by Aurea or ESW Capital; while Aurea and its affiliate ESW Capital are known for acquiring and operating a portfolio of enterprise software brands (such as Jive Software), no documentation was found directly linking Autopilot or Ortto to that portfolio, so any claim of an Aurea or ESW Capital acquisition should be treated as unverified rather than confirmed fact. What is verifiable is that Autopilot no longer exists as an actively sold, independently branded product: its technology, customer base, and go-to-market direction continued forward under the Ortto brand starting in 2022, and prospective buyers researching Autopilot today should evaluate Ortto instead, since that is where current pricing, features, and support are found. Because Autopilot itself is discontinued as a brand, this profile should be read as historical and transitional context rather than a live product recommendation; for current features and pricing, readers should consult Ortto directly rather than relying on legacy Autopilot marketing materials, which may be outdated or no longer maintained.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Was Autopilot
paragraphs: Autopilot was a visual marketing automation platform known for its drag-and-drop canvas interface, which let marketers build multi-step customer journeys, automated emails, and lead-nurturing sequences without writing code. The company was founded around 2011 and grew a customer base among small and mid-sized marketing teams looking for an alternative to more complex enterprise marketing automation suites.

In 2021, the company undertook a significant product rebuild, expanding beyond journey-based marketing automation into broader customer data management and analytics, releasing the new platform in beta that year.

In March 2022, the company completed a full rebrand from Autopilot to Ortto, describing the change as a natural evolution of the product and company rather than an acquisition-driven shutdown; the autopilothq.com domain now redirects visitors to Ortto's website.

long_form_blocks.heading: Ownership and Rebrand History
paragraphs: Autopilot's own public materials attribute the Ortto rebrand to organic company growth and an expanded product vision, positioning Ortto as encompassing customer data, messaging, and analytics in a single platform rather than just the original journey-builder tool.

Some third-party sources reference Aurea and its affiliate ESW Capital as a firm known for acquiring and consolidating enterprise software brands, including companies like Jive Software. However, available public sources for this research did not surface a documented, verifiable connection between Autopilot or Ortto and Aurea or ESW Capital, so this should not be treated as confirmed; readers who have seen this claim elsewhere should independently verify it against primary sources such as SEC filings, press releases, or Ortto's own corporate disclosures.

What can be confirmed is that Autopilot is no longer sold or marketed under its original name. The product, customers, and underlying technology continued forward as Ortto, meaning any evaluation of 'Autopilot' today should really be an evaluation of Ortto's current offering, pricing, and roadmap.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing Today
paragraphs: Because Autopilot has been fully retired as a brand since March 2022, there is no current, independently priced Autopilot product; all pricing today is set and published by Ortto.
Historical Autopilot pricing information is not reliable for purchasing decisions since the underlying product, plans, and pricing structure changed substantially with the Ortto rebuild and rebrand.

Prospective buyers interested in the capabilities Autopilot originally offered should request current pricing directly from Ortto rather than relying on legacy Autopilot marketing pages, which may no longer reflect the live product.

features.description: Campaign and journey performance reporting, expanded significantly under the Ortto rebuild.

Underlying technology and customer relationships carried forward into Ortto rather than being shut down
Rebrand added broader customer data and analytics capabilities beyond the original tool
Established company history dating back to the early 2010s
cons: Autopilot as a brand and standalone product no longer exists
Legacy marketing materials and reviews referencing Autopilot may be outdated
Migration to Ortto's rebuilt platform may have changed pricing and features from what long-time Autopilot users expected
Ownership history involving any Aurea or ESW Capital connection could not be independently confirmed from available sources

faqs.question: Is Autopilot marketing automation still available
faqs.answer: No, Autopilot was fully rebranded as Ortto in March 2022, and the autopilothq.com domain now redirects to Ortto's website.

faqs.question: What happened to Autopilot
faqs.answer: Autopilot's company rebuilt its product with expanded customer data and analytics features starting in 2021 and completed a full rebrand to Ortto in March 2022.

faqs.question: Was Autopilot acquired by Aurea or ESW Capital
faqs.answer: This research did not find a verifiable public source confirming an acquisition of Autopilot by Aurea or ESW Capital; this claim should be independently verified before being treated as fact.

faqs.question: Where can I find Autopilot's current pricing
faqs.answer: Because Autopilot no longer exists as an independently branded product, current pricing is published by Ortto, its successor platform.

faqs.question: Is Ortto the same company as Autopilot
faqs.answer: Yes, Ortto is the rebranded continuation of Autopilot, using the same underlying technology and customer base under an expanded product vision.

faqs.question: When was Autopilot founded
faqs.answer: Autopilot was founded around 2011, though exact incorporation details are not comprehensively documented in public sources.

faqs.question: Should I sign up for Autopilot today
faqs.answer: No, prospective buyers should evaluate Ortto directly, since Autopilot is no longer sold or supported under its original name.

enrichment.short_description: Avail is a property management platform for independent landlords, offering free and paid tools for listing rentals, screening tenants, signing leases, and collecting rent; it operates as part of the Realtor.com network following its 2020 acquisition by Move, Inc.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Unlimited plan); paid Unlimited Plus plan is $9 per unit per month
enrichment.company_size: operates as part of Move, Inc. (Realtor.com), a News Corp subsidiary
enrichment.headquarters: Chicago, Illinois, United States (as part of the Realtor.com network)
enrichment.seo_title: Avail Review 2026: Pricing, Features for DIY Landlords
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Avail is Realtor.com's landlord software for listing, screening, leasing and rent collection. See 2026 pricing, features, pros and cons.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Avail is a property management platform built for independent, do-it-yourself landlords who typically own between one and fifty rental units. It began as Rentalutions, a Chicago-based startup founded around 2013, before rebranding to Avail. On December 15, 2020, Move, Inc., the operator of Realtor.com and a subsidiary of News Corp, acquired Avail; the acquisition's financial terms were not publicly disclosed. Following the deal, Avail continued operating under its own brand rather than being absorbed or discontinued, and it is now marketed as 'Avail, by Realtor.com,' reflecting its position within the larger Realtor.com rental products ecosystem while retaining its distinct product identity. In 2026, Avail began migrating its web presence from the avail.co domain to avail.com to better align with its Realtor.com branding, with the old domain redirecting automatically. The platform provides landlords with tools to list rental properties across multiple listing sites (Avail advertises distribution to roughly 19 rental listing sites), screen prospective tenants with reports powered by TransUnion (including credit, criminal background, and eviction history), generate state- and city-specific digital lease agreements, collect rent online, and track maintenance requests through a tenant portal. Avail's pricing follows a freemium model: the Unlimited plan is free and includes core listing, screening, leasing, and rent collection features, while the paid Unlimited Plus plan costs nine dollars per unit per month and adds features such as faster rent payouts (FastPay), waived ACH transfer fees, customizable lease templates, a brandable property website, and priority customer support; tenants may separately pay for screening reports and a card payment processing fee of 3.5 percent applies on both plans. Avail's target audience is small-scale, independent landlords who want professional-grade tools without the cost or complexity of full-service property management companies. It competes with platforms such as TurboTenant, Zillow Rental Manager, RentRedi, and Buildium (the last more oriented toward larger portfolios), differentiating itself through its integration with the high-traffic Realtor.com listing network and its ownership by a major, publicly traded media and real estate company.

long_form_blocks.heading: What Is Avail
paragraphs: Avail is a property management platform designed for independent landlords, typically those managing between one and fifty rental units, who want professional tools for listing, screening, leasing, and rent collection without hiring a full-service property manager. The company began as Rentalutions, a Chicago-based startup founded around 2013, before rebranding to Avail.

On December 15, 2020, Move, Inc., the company behind Realtor.com and a subsidiary of News Corp, acquired Avail. Rather than folding Avail into Realtor.com's broader brand or discontinuing it, the acquisition kept Avail operating under its own name, now marketed as 'Avail, by Realtor.com,' reflecting its place inside the larger Realtor.com family of products while retaining a distinct identity aimed specifically at DIY landlords.

In 2026, Avail began transitioning its primary web domain from avail.co to avail.com to align more closely with the Realtor.com brand family; the legacy avail.co domain now redirects automatically to avail.com.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Avail lets landlords list rental properties across roughly 19 rental listing sites from a single dashboard, reducing the manual work of posting to multiple platforms individually.

For tenant screening, Avail partners with TransUnion to provide credit reports, criminal background checks, and eviction history as part of the applicant review process, and it offers state- and city-specific digital lease agreements that landlords can customize and send for e-signature.

Once a lease is signed, landlords can collect rent online, track maintenance requests submitted through a 24/7 tenant portal, and use built-in financial tracking and messaging tools to manage day-to-day communication with tenants.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Avail's core Unlimited plan is free per unit and includes listing distribution, tenant applications and screening, state-specific leases, online rent collection, maintenance tracking, and tenant portal access.

The paid Unlimited Plus plan costs nine dollars per unit per month and adds faster rent payouts through FastPay, waived ACH transfer fees, customizable and clonable lease templates, a branded property marketing website, and priority customer support with faster response times.

Outside the core subscription, tenants may be charged separately for screening reports depending on location, and a 3.5 percent fee applies to card payments on both the free and paid plans; bank transfer fees are $2.50 on the free plan and waived on Unlimited Plus.

features.title: Multi-site rental listing
features.description: Distributes property listings to roughly 19 rental listing sites from a single dashboard.

features.title: Tenant screening
features.description: Provides credit, criminal background, and eviction reports powered by TransUnion.

features.title: State-specific digital leases
features.description: Generates state- and city-specific lease agreements with e-signature support.

features.title: Online rent collection
features.description: Lets tenants pay rent online via bank transfer or card, with optional FastPay payout speed.

features.title: Maintenance request tracking
features.description: Tenants submit and track maintenance requests through a 24/7 online portal.

features.title: Financial tracking tools
features.description: Helps landlords track rental income and expenses within the platform.

features.title: Branded property website
features.description: Available on the paid plan, lets landlords create a custom-branded marketing page for their rentals.

pros: Core plan is free, covering listing, screening, leasing, and rent collection
Backed by Realtor.com's large rental listing network and audience
Tenant screening powered by an established credit bureau, TransUnion
State- and city-specific lease templates reduce legal guesswork for landlords
Purpose-built for small-scale independent landlords rather than large portfolios
cons: Card payment processing carries a 3.5 percent fee on both plans
Free plan charges a per-transfer fee that only the paid plan waives
Best suited to small portfolios (one to fifty units) rather than large-scale property management
Some advanced features are gated behind the paid Unlimited Plus tier
Domain and branding transition from avail.co to avail.com may cause temporary confusion for existing users

faqs.question: Is Avail free for landlords
faqs.answer: Yes, Avail's Unlimited plan is free per unit and includes listing, tenant screening, leases, rent collection, and maintenance tracking; a paid Unlimited Plus plan adds extra features for nine dollars per unit per month.

faqs.question: Who owns Avail
faqs.answer: Avail is owned by Move, Inc., the operator of Realtor.com, which acquired it on December 15, 2020; Move is a subsidiary of News Corp.

faqs.question: Did Avail rebrand or merge into Realtor.com
faqs.answer: Avail continues to operate under its own brand as 'Avail, by Realtor.com' rather than being fully merged or discontinued, though it moved its website from avail.co to avail.com in 2026.

faqs.question: How many rental units can Avail handle
faqs.answer: Avail is designed primarily for independent landlords managing between one and fifty rental units.

faqs.question: Does Avail screen tenants
faqs.answer: Yes, Avail provides tenant screening reports, including credit, criminal background, and eviction history, powered by TransUnion.

faqs.question: What is included in Avail Unlimited Plus
faqs.answer: Unlimited Plus adds faster rent payouts through FastPay, waived ACH fees, customizable lease templates, a branded property website, and priority support for nine dollars per unit per month.

faqs.question: Is Avail the same company as Rentalutions
faqs.answer: Yes, Avail began as Rentalutions, a Chicago-based startup founded around 2013, before rebranding to Avail.

faqs.question: What fees does Avail charge for rent payments
faqs.answer: Card payments carry a 3.5 percent fee on both plans; bank transfers cost $2.50 on the free plan and are free on the Unlimited Plus plan.

use_cases.title: First-time landlord listing a rental
use_cases.description: New landlords use Avail's free plan to list a property across multiple rental sites and screen applicants without hiring a property manager.

use_cases.title: Landlord managing multiple properties
use_cases.description: Landlords with several units use Avail's Unlimited Plus plan for faster payouts, branded listings, and priority support across their portfolio.

use_cases.title: Landlord needing state-compliant leases
use_cases.description: Landlords in states with specific legal requirements use Avail's state- and city-specific lease templates to stay compliant.
